label: Post-Chemo Fatigue — Low-Dose Naltrexone

sources.name: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) fatigue/fibromyalgia trials
sources.note: Small RCTs and crossover studies of LDN for fatigue and chronic pain; use for plausible effect size and safety, labeled as small-sample evidence.

sources.name: Cancer-related fatigue measurement instruments (BFI, FACIT-F, PROMIS Fatigue)
sources.note: Validated patient-reported fatigue endpoints. Use to define a defensible primary outcome and minimal clinically important difference (MCID).

sources.name: NCCN Cancer-Related Fatigue guideline
sources.note: Defines cancer-related fatigue and standard management. Use for population definition and standard-of-care comparator context.

knownFacts: Population: breast cancer survivors 6-24 months post-chemotherapy completion.
Primary endpoint as stated: >=30% reduction in cancer-related fatigue severity score.
Cancer-related fatigue is multifactorial (anemia, deconditioning, depression, sleep, inflammation), which complicates attributing change to a single agent.
quantContext: Fatigue PRO scales have meaningful placebo response rates; a 30% responder threshold must account for placebo response to avoid an underpowered or biased comparison.
MCID on common fatigue instruments is often smaller than a 30% relative change; choice of responder definition strongly affects required sample size.
12 weeks may be short relative to the natural recovery trajectory of post-chemo fatigue, risking confounding by spontaneous improvement.
demoCaveats: LDN evidence base for cancer-related fatigue is limited and mostly small-sample — do not overstate prior efficacy.
Any stated effect size or sample-size figure is an estimate unless explicitly sourced.
Spontaneous recovery over 6-24 months post-chemo is a real confounder to address, not to ignore.
